The commission shall not make the report available to any person other than the person who was the subject of the criminal records check; an appointing or licensing authority; a member, the executive director, or an employee of the commission; or any court or agency, including a hearing examiner, in a judicial or administrative proceeding relating to the person's employment or application for a license under this chapter.

Sec. 3772.09. (A) No casino operator, management company, holding company, gaming-related vendor, key gaming employee, or casino gaming employee shall conduct or participate in conducting casino gaming without first obtaining a license from the commission.

(B) Before a licensed casino operator may conduct casino gaming at a casino facility, a licensed casino operator shall engage a third-party engineering or accounting firm to certify expenses of its initial investment, as required by section 3772.27 of the Revised Code, and provide documentation to the commission. The third-party engineering or accounting firm shall be approved by the commission and shall certify expenses in accordance with rules adopted by the commission under section 3772.03 of the Revised Code. The commission may request the department of administrative services to assist the commission in carrying out its duties under this section.

Sec. 3772.091. (A) No license issued under this chapter is transferable. Except as provided in division (B) of this section, new majority ownership interest or control of a licensee shall require a new license. The commission may reopen a licensing investigation at any time. A significant change in or transfer of control, as determined by the commission, shall require the filing of an application for a new license and submission of a license fee with the commission before any such change or transfer of control is approved. A change in or transfer of control to an immediate family member is not considered a significant change under this section.

(B) An initial license shall not be considered transferred, and a new license shall not be required, when an initial licensee that is licensed before June 1, 2013, does or has done both of the following:

(1) Obtains a majority ownership interest in, or a change in or transfer of control of, another initial licensee for the same casino facility; and

(2) Was investigated under this chapter as a parent, affiliate, subsidiary, key gaming employee, or partner, or joint venturer with another initial licensee that has held for the same casino facility a majority ownership interest in or control of the initial license when the initial license was issued and when such an initial licensee obtains a majority ownership interest in or a change in or transfer of control.

(C) As used in this section:

(1) "Control" means either of the following:

(a) Either:

(i) Holding fifty per cent or more of the outstanding voting securities of a licensee; or

(ii) For an unincorporated licensee, having the right to fifty per cent or more of the profits of the licensee, or having the right in the event of dissolution to fifty per cent or more of the assets of the licensee.

(b) Having the contractual power presently to designate fifty per cent or more of the directors of a for-profit or not-for-profit corporation, or in the case of trusts described in paragraphs (c)(3) to (5) of 16 C.F.R. 801.1, the trustees of such a trust.

(2) "Initial license" means the first plenary license issued to an initial licensee.

(3) "Initial licensee" means any of the persons issued an initial license to conduct or participate in conducting casino gaming at each casino facility as either a casino operator, a management company, or a holding company of a casino operator or management company.

Sec. 3772.10. (A) In determining whether to grant or maintain the privilege of a casino operator, management company, holding company, key employee, casino gaming employee, or gaming-related vendor license, the Ohio casino control commission shall, except as provided in division (D) of this section, consider all of the following, as applicable:

(1) The reputation, experience, and financial integrity of the applicant, its holding company, if applicable, and any other person that directly or indirectly controls the applicant;

(2) The financial ability of the applicant to purchase and maintain adequate liability and casualty insurance and to provide an adequate surety bond;

(3) The past and present compliance of the applicant and its affiliates or affiliated companies with casino-related licensing requirements in this state or any other jurisdiction, including whether the applicant has a history of noncompliance with the casino licensing requirements of any jurisdiction;

(4) If the applicant has been indicted, convicted, pleaded guilty or no contest, or forfeited bail concerning any criminal offense under the laws of any jurisdiction, either felony or misdemeanor, not including traffic violations;

(5) If the applicant has filed, or had filed against it a proceeding for bankruptcy or has ever been involved in any formal process to adjust, defer, suspend, or otherwise work out the payment of any debt;

(6) If the applicant has been served with a complaint or other notice filed with any public body regarding a payment of any tax required under federal, state, or local law that has been delinquent for one or more years;

(7) If the applicant is or has been a defendant in litigation involving its business practices;

(8) If awarding a license would undermine the public's confidence in the casino gaming industry in this state;

(9) If the applicant meets other standards for the issuance of a license that the commission adopts by rule, which shall not be arbitrary, capricious, or contradictory to the expressed provisions of this chapter.

(B) All applicants for a license under this chapter shall establish their suitability for a license by clear and convincing evidence. If the commission determines that a person is eligible under this chapter to be issued a license as a casino operator, management company, holding company, key employee, casino gaming employee, or gaming-related vendor, the commission shall issue such license for not more than three years, as determined by commission rule, if all other requirements of this chapter have been satisfied.

(C) The commission shall not, except as provided in division (D) of this section, issue a casino operator, management company, holding company, key employee, casino gaming employee, or gaming-related vendor license under this chapter to an applicant if:

(1) The applicant has been convicted of a disqualifying offense, as defined in section 3772.07 of the Revised Code.

(2) The applicant has submitted an application for license under this chapter that contains false information.

(3) The applicant is a commission member.

(4) The applicant owns an ownership interest that is unlawful under this chapter, unless waived by the commission.

(5) The applicant violates specific rules adopted by the commission related to denial of licensure.

(6) The applicant is a member of or employed by a gaming regulatory body of a governmental unit in this state, another state, or the federal government, or is an employee of a governmental unit of this state and in that capacity has significant influence or control, as determined by the commission, over the ability of a casino operator, management company, holding company, institutional investor, or gaming-related vendor to conduct business in this state. This division does not prohibit a casino operator or management company from hiring special duty law enforcement officers if the officers are not specifically involved in gaming-related regulatory functions.

(7) The commission otherwise determines the applicant is ineligible for the license.

(D) The commission shall not refuse to issue a license to an applicant because the applicant was convicted of or pleaded guilty to an offense unless the refusal is in accordance with section 9.79 of the Revised Code.

(E)(1) The commission shall investigate the qualifications of each applicant under this chapter before any license is issued and before any finding with regard to acts or transactions for which commission approval is required is made. The commission shall continue to observe the conduct of all licensees and all other persons having a material involvement directly or indirectly with a casino operator, management company, or holding company to ensure that licenses are not issued to or held by, or that there is not any material involvement with a casino operator, management company, or holding company by, an unqualified, disqualified, or unsuitable person or a person whose operations are conducted in an unsuitable manner or in unsuitable or prohibited places or locations.

(2) The executive director may recommend to the commission that it deny any application, or limit, condition, or restrict, or suspend or revoke, any license or finding, or impose any fine upon any licensee or other person according to this chapter and the rules adopted thereunder.

(3) A license issued under this chapter is a revocable privilege. No licensee has a vested right in or under any license issued under this chapter. The initial determination of the commission to deny, or to limit, condition, or restrict, a license may be appealed under section 2505.03 of the Revised Code.

(4) In reviewing an application for a license under this chapter, the commission may rely on or consider the last investigation it conducted concerning the applicant, or the last license it issued to the applicant, under this chapter or Chapter 3770., 3771., or 3775. of the Revised Code.

(F)(1) An institutional investor may be found to be suitable or qualified by the commission under this chapter and the rules adopted under this chapter. An institutional investor shall be presumed suitable or qualified upon submitting documentation sufficient to establish qualifications as an institutional investor and upon certifying all of the following:

(a) The institutional investor owns, holds, or controls securities issued by a licensee or holding, intermediate, or parent company of a licensee or in the ordinary course of business for investment purposes only.

(b) The institutional investor does not exercise influence over the affairs of the issuer of such securities nor over any licensed subsidiary of the issuer of such securities.

(c) The institutional investor does not intend to exercise influence over the affairs of the issuer of such securities, nor over any licensed subsidiary of the issuer of such securities, in the future, and that it agrees to notify the commission in writing within thirty days if such intent changes.

(2) The exercise of voting privileges with regard to securities shall not be deemed to constitute the exercise of influence over the affairs of a licensee.

(3) The commission shall rescind the presumption of suitability for an institutional investor at any time if the institutional investor exercises or intends to exercise influence or control over the affairs of the licensee.

(4) This division shall not be construed to preclude the commission from requesting information from or investigating the suitability or qualifications of an institutional investor if:

(a) The commission becomes aware of facts or information that may result in the institutional investor being found unsuitable or disqualified; or

(b) The commission has any other reason to seek information from the investor to determine whether it qualifies as an institutional investor.

(5) If the commission finds an institutional investor to be unsuitable or unqualified, the commission shall so notify the investor and the casino operator, holding company, management company, or gaming-related vendor licensee in which the investor invested. The commission shall allow the investor and the licensee a reasonable amount of time, as specified by the commission on a case-by-case basis, to cure the conditions that caused the commission to find the investor unsuitable or unqualified. If during the specified period of time the investor or the licensee does not or cannot cure the conditions that caused the commission to find the investor unsuitable or unqualified, the commission may allow the investor or licensee more time to cure the conditions or the commission may begin proceedings to deny, suspend, or revoke the license of the casino operator, holding company, management company, or gaming-related vendor in which the investor invested or to deny any of the same the renewal of any such license.

(6) A private licensee or holding company shall provide the same information to the commission as a public company would provide in a form 13d or form 13g filing to the securities and exchange commission.

(G) Information provided on the application shall be used as a basis for a thorough background investigation of each applicant. A false or incomplete application is cause for denial of a license by the commission. All applicants and licensees shall consent to inspections, searches, and seizures and to the disclosure to the commission and its agents of confidential records, including tax records, held by any federal, state, or local agency, credit bureau, or financial institution and to provide handwriting exemplars, photographs, fingerprints, and information as authorized in this chapter and in rules adopted by the commission.

(H) The commission shall provide a written statement to each applicant for a license under this chapter who is denied the license that describes the reason or reasons for which the applicant was denied the license.

(I) Not later than January 31 in each calendar year, the commission shall provide to the general assembly and the governor a report that, for each type of license issued under this chapter, specifies the number of applications made in the preceding calendar year for each type of such license, the number of applications denied in the preceding calendar year for each type of such license, and the reasons for those denials. The information regarding the reasons for the denials shall specify each reason that resulted in, or that was a factor resulting in, denial for each type of license issued under this chapter and, for each of those reasons, the total number of denials for each such type that involved that reason.

Sec. 3772.113. (A) An applicant or licensee under this chapter or Chapter 3769., 3771., or 3775. or sections 3770.21 to 3770.22 of the Revised Code shall notify the commission in writing within ten days of any material change to any information the person provided in an application for an initial or renewed license, including all of the following:

(1) Any change of name;

(2) Any change of home or business address;

(3) Any change of primary telephone number or electronic mail address;

(4) Any change to the person's state of incorporation or principal place of business;

(5) Any change in the ownership or control of the person;

(6) Any bankruptcy filed, discharged, or dismissed;

(7) Any arrest, charge, plea, or conviction for any crime or offense occurring in any jurisdiction, excluding minor misdemeanor traffic offenses;

(8) Any investigation commenced by or action filed by a gaming regulatory agency or government gaming authority, except for routine renewal application reviews;

(9) Any rejection, suspension, revocation, or denial of any gaming-related application or license, and any fine, penalty, or settled amount related to any gaming-related license imposed upon or agreed to in any jurisdiction;

(10) Any other changes designated on an applicable update form prescribed by the commission;

(11) Any other information affecting the person's suitability for the license;

(12) Any other information required by the commission by rule.

(B)(1) A licensee that is not an individual shall display its license conspicuously in its place of business or shall make the license available for inspection by the commission or any law enforcement agency upon request.

(2) A key gaming employee or gaming employee who is present at a track, casino facility, or sports gaming facility shall prominently display evidence of the person's license, in a manner determined by the commission, while actively performing the person's duties.

Sec. 3772.13. (A) No person may be employed as a key employee of a casino operator, management company, or holding company unless the person is the holder of a valid key employee license issued by the commission. Any executive, employee, agent, or other individual who, as determined by the commission, has the power to exercise significant influence over decisions concerning any part of the operation of a person that is licensed as any of the following shall hold a valid key gaming employee license issued by the commission:

(1) A permit holder, management services company, or totalizator company, for purposes of operating under Chapter 3769. of the Revised Code;

(2) A video lottery sales agent, for purposes of operating under sections 3770.21 to 3770.22 of the Revised Code;

(3) An internet gambling operator, internet gambling management company, or internet gambling supplier, for purposes of operating under Chapter 3771. of the Revised Code;

(4) A casino operator, management company, or gaming-related vendor or a holding company of a casino operator, management company, or gaming-related vendor, for purposes of operating under this chapter;

(5) A sports gaming proprietor other than a professional sports organization, a mobile management services provider, a management services provider, or a sports gaming supplier, for purposes of operating under Chapter 3775. of the Revised Code.

(B) No person may be employed as a key employee of a gaming-related vendor unless that person is either the holder of a valid key gaming employee license issued by the commission, or the person, at least five business days prior to the first day of employment as a key employee, has filed a notification of employment with the commission and subsequently files a completed application for a key gaming employee license within the first thirty days of employment as a key employee.

(C) Each applicant for a key gaming employee license shall, before the issuance of any key employee license, produce the information, documentation, and assurances as are required by this chapter and rules adopted thereunder. In addition, each applicant shall, in writing, authorize the examination of all bank accounts and records as may be deemed necessary by the commission.

(D) To be eligible for a key gaming employee license, the applicant shall be at least twenty-one years of age and shall meet the criteria set forth by rule by the commission.

(E) Each application for a key gaming employee license shall be on a form prescribed by the commission and shall contain all information required by the commission. The applicant shall set forth in the application if the applicant has been issued prior gambling-related licenses; if the applicant has been licensed in any other state under any other name, and, if so, the name under which the license was issued and the applicant's age at the time the license was issued; any criminal conviction the applicant has had; and if a permit or license issued to the applicant in any other state has been suspended, restricted, or revoked, and, if so, the cause and the duration of each action. The applicant also shall complete a cover sheet for the application on which the applicant shall disclose the applicant's name, the business address and telephone number of the casino operator, management company, holding company, or gaming-related vendor person employing the applicant, the business address and telephone number of such employer, and the county, state, and country in which the applicant's residence is located.

(F) Each applicant shall submit with each application, on a form provided by the commission, two sets of fingerprints. The commission shall charge each applicant an the applicant's employer the fee required under division (C)(3) of section 109.572 of the Revised Code, along with a nonrefundable application fee of not more than one thousand seven hundred fifty dollars, as set by the commission, to cover the actual costs generated by each licensee and the background checks under this section and section 3772.07 of the Revised Code. Upon issuing the license, the commission shall charge the applicant's employer a nonrefundable license fee of fifty dollars.

(G)(1) The casino operator, management company, or holding company person by whom a person is employed as a key gaming employee shall terminate the person's employment in any capacity requiring a key gaming employee license under this chapter and shall not in any manner permit the person to exercise a significant influence over the operation of a casino facility person's operations if:

(a) The person does not apply for and receive a key gaming employee license within three months of being issued a provisional license, as established under commission rule.

(b) The person's application for a key gaming employee license is denied by the commission.

(c) The person's key gaming employee license is revoked by the commission.

The commission shall notify the casino operator, management company, or holding company who employs such a person employer by certified mail, personal service, common carrier service utilizing any form of delivery requiring a signed receipt or by an electronic means that provides evidence of delivery, of any such finding, denial, or revocation.

(2) A casino operator, management company, or holding company An employer shall not pay to a person whose employment is terminated under division (G)(1) of this section, any remuneration for any services performed in any capacity in which the person is required to be licensed as a key gaming employee, except for amounts due for services rendered before notice was received under that division. A contract or other agreement for personal services or for the conduct of any casino gaming at a casino facility between a casino operator, management company, or holding company an employer and a person whose employment is terminated under division (G)(1) of this section may be terminated by the casino operator, management company, or holding company employer without further liability on the part of the casino operator, management company, or holding company employer. Any such contract or other agreement is deemed to include a term authorizing its termination without further liability on the part of the casino operator, management company, or holding company employer upon receiving notice under division (G)(1) of this section. That a contract or other agreement does not expressly include such a term is not a defense in any action brought to terminate the contract or other agreement, and is not grounds for relief in any action brought questioning termination of the contract or other agreement.

(3) A casino operator, management company, or holding company person described in divisions (A)(1) to (5) of this section, without having obtained the prior approval of the commission, shall not enter into any contract or other agreement with a person who has been found unsuitable, who has been denied a key gaming employee license, or whose license has been revoked under division (G)(1) of this section, or with any business enterprise under the control of such a person, after the date on which the casino operator, management company, or holding company person described in divisions (A)(1) to (5) of this section receives notice under that division.

(H) Notwithstanding the requirements for a license under this section, the commission shall issue a key gaming employee license in accordance with Chapter 4796. of the Revised Code to an applicant if either of the following applies:

(1) The applicant holds a license in another state.

(2) The applicant has satisfactory work experience, a government certification, or a private certification as described in that chapter as a key gaming employee of a casino operator, management company, or holding company in a state that does not issue that license.

(I) A key gaming employee license is valid for a term of three years. In order to renew a key gaming employee license, the licensee shall apply to the commission for a renewed license in the same manner as for an initial license, except as otherwise provided by the commission by rule.

(J) An individual who is required under this section to hold a key gaming employee license by reason of the individual's role with respect to more than one type of gaming is required to hold only one key gaming employee license.

Sec. 3772.131. (A) All casino gaming employees are required to have An individual whose duties are such that the individual has the ability to alter material aspects of pari-mutuel wagering on horse racing, video lottery terminal gaming, internet gambling, casino gaming, or sports gaming, including any of the following duties, shall hold a casino valid gaming employee license. "Casino gaming employee" means the following and their supervisors issued by the commission at all times:

(1) Individuals involved in operating Operating a casino gaming pit or casino games, including dealers, shills, clerks, hosts, and junket representatives;

(2) Individuals involved in handling Accepting wagers of any kind;

(3) Handling money as part of a gaming operation, including cashiers, change persons, count teams, and coin wrappers;

(3) Individuals involved in operating casino games;

(4) Individuals involved in operating Operating and maintaining slot machines, including mechanics, floor persons, and change and payoff persons, or operating and maintaining a totalizator, video lottery terminal, internet gambling equipment, or sports gaming equipment;

(5) Individuals involved in (4) Providing security for any gaming operation, including guards and game observers, other than providing general security at a type C sports gaming host's facility;

(6) Individuals with (5) Performing duties similar to those described in divisions (A)(1) to (5) (4) of this section or other persons ;

(6) Performing other duties, as the commission determines. "Casino gaming employee" does not include an

(B)(1) An individual whose is not required to have a gaming employee license if the individual has a key gaming employee license.

(2) An individual is not required to have a gaming employee license if the individual's duties are related solely to nongaming activities such as entertainment, hotel operation, maintenance, or preparing or serving food and beverages, including an individual who is, or is employed by, a type C sports gaming host.

(B) (C) The commission may issue a casino gaming employee license to an applicant after it has determined that the applicant is eligible for a license under rules adopted by the commission and paid any applicable fee set by the commission by rule in the amount necessary to cover the commission's costs associated with the review of the application. The applicant's employer shall pay all application and license fees on behalf of the applicant. All applications shall be certified as true.

(C) (D) To be eligible for a casino gaming employee license, an applicant shall be at least twenty-one years of age.

(D) (E) Each application for a casino gaming employee license shall be on a form prescribed by the commission and shall contain all information required by the commission. The applicant shall set forth in the application if the applicant has been issued prior gambling-related licenses; if the applicant has been licensed in any other state under any other name, and, if so, the name under which the license was issued and the applicant's age at the time the license was issued; any criminal conviction the applicant has had; and if a permit or license issued to the applicant in any other state has been suspended, restricted, or revoked, and, if so, the cause and the duration of each action.

(E) (F) Each applicant shall submit with each application, on a form provided by the commission, two sets of the applicant's fingerprints. The commission shall charge each applicant an application the applicant's employer the fee to cover all actual costs generated by each licensee and all background checks required under division (C)(3) of section 109.572 of the Revised Code, along with a nonrefundable application fee of one hundred dollars. Upon issuing the license, the commission shall charge the applicant's employer a nonrefundable license fee of fifty dollars.

(F) (G) Notwithstanding the requirements for a license under this section, the commission shall issue a casino gaming employee license in accordance with Chapter 4796. of the Revised Code to an applicant if either of the following applies:

(1) The applicant holds a license in another state.

(2) The applicant has satisfactory work experience, a government certification, or a private certification as described in that chapter as a casino gaming employee in a state that does not issue that license.

(H) A gaming employee license is valid for a term of three years. In order to renew a gaming employee license, the licensee shall apply to the commission for a renewed license in the same manner as for an initial license, except as otherwise provided by the commission by rule.

(I) An individual who is required under this section to hold a gaming employee license by reason of the individual's duties with respect to more than one type of gaming is required to hold only one gaming employee license.

Sec. 3772.132. (A) An applicant for a key gaming employee or gaming employee license shall establish the applicant's suitability for the license by clear and convincing evidence.

(B) The commission shall not grant a key gaming employee or gaming employee license to an applicant if evidence satisfactory to the commission exists that the applicant has done any of the following:

(1) Knowingly made a false statement to the commission;

(2) Been suspended from operating a gambling game, gaming device, or gaming operation, or had a license revoked by any governmental unit of a national, state, or local body exercising governmental functions;

(3) Been convicted of a disqualifying offense, as defined in section 3772.07 of the Revised Code;

(4) Been directly involved in or employed by any offshore wagering market that illegally serviced the United States or otherwise accepted illegal wagers from individuals located in the United States on or after April 16, 2015.

(C) The commission shall not grant a key gaming employee or gaming employee license to an individual to whom any of the following apply:

(1) The individual is under twenty-one years of age;

(2) The individual is a member or employee of the commission or of the state lottery commission;

(3) The individual is a member or employee of a gaming regulatory body of another state or the federal government;

(4) The individual is an employee of a governmental unit of this state who, in that capacity, has significant influence or control, as determined by the commission, over the ability of a person described in divisions (A)(1) to (5) of section 3772.13 of the Revised Code to conduct business in this state. This division does not prohibit a special duty law enforcement officer from receiving a gaming employee license if the officer is not specifically involved in gaming-related regulatory functions.

(5) The commission otherwise determines the individual is ineligible for the license.

(D) In determining whether to grant, maintain, or renew a key gaming employee or gaming employee license, the commission shall evaluate and consider all of the following factors:

(1) Whether the individual possesses good character, honesty, and integrity;

(2) Whether the individual possesses financial stability, integrity, and responsibility;

(3) The criminal history of the individual in any jurisdiction;

(4) Whether the individual has filed or had filed against the individual a proceeding for bankruptcy or has ever been involved in any formal process to adjust, defer, suspend, or otherwise resolve the payment of any debt in the last ten years;

(5) Whether the individual has been served with a complaint or other notice filed with any public body regarding any payment of tax required under federal, state, or local law that has been delinquent for one or more years in the last ten years;

(6) Whether the individual is a party to any currently pending litigation or has been a defendant in litigation during the last ten years;

(7) The extent to which the individual has cooperated with the commission in connection with the background investigation;

(8) The extent to which the individual has provided accurate and complete information as required by the commission;

(9) Whether the individual has been issued a comparable license in another jurisdiction with similar licensing requirements, as determined by the commission, including the individual's related compliance history;

(10) The extent to which the individual has complied with the requirements of the Revised Code with respect to gaming and the rules adopted thereunder.

(E) In reviewing an application for a license, the commission may rely on or consider the last investigation it conducted concerning the applicant, or the last license it issued to the applicant, under this chapter or Chapter 3770., 3771., or 3775. of the Revised Code.

Sec. 3772.15. (A) Unless a license issued under this chapter is suspended, expires, or is revoked, the license shall be renewed for three years, as determined by commission rule, after a determination by the commission that the licensee is in compliance with this chapter and rules authorized by this chapter and after the licensee pays a fee. The commission may assess the license renewal applicant a reasonable fee in the amount necessary to cover the commission's costs associated with the review of the license renewal application.

(B) A licensee shall undergo a complete investigation at least every three years, as determined by commission rule, to determine that the licensee remains in compliance with this chapter.

(C) Notwithstanding division (B) of this section, the commission may investigate a licensee at any time the commission determines it is necessary to ensure that the licensee remains in compliance with this section.

(D) The holder of a license shall bear the cost of an investigation, except that the employer shall pay the investigation cost for key gaming employees and casino gaming employees who are employed by a casino operator, in which case the casino operator shall pay the investigation cost.

Sec. 3772.16. (A) Any information concerning the following submitted, collected, or gathered as part of an application to the commission for a license under this chapter is confidential and not subject to disclosure by any state agency or political subdivision as a record under section 149.43 of the Revised Code:

(1) A minor child of an applicant;

(2) The social security number, passport number, or federal tax identification number of an applicant or the spouse of an applicant;

(3) The home address and telephone number of an applicant or the spouse or dependent of an applicant;

(4) An applicant's birth certificate;

(5) The driver's license number of an applicant or the applicant's spouse;

(6) The name or address of a previous spouse of the applicant;

(7) The date of birth of the applicant and the spouse of an applicant;

(8) The place of birth of the applicant and the spouse of an applicant;

(9) The personal financial information and records of an applicant or of an employee or the spouse or dependent of an applicant, including tax returns and information, and records of criminal proceedings;

(10) Any information concerning a victim of domestic violence, sexual assault, or stalking;

(11) The electronic mail address of the spouse or family member of the applicant;

(12) Any trade secret, medical records, and patents or exclusive licenses;

(13) Security information, including risk prevention plans, detection and countermeasures, location of count rooms or other money storage areas, emergency management plans, security and surveillance plans, equipment and usage protocols, and theft and fraud prevention plans and countermeasures;

(14) Information provided in a multijurisdictional personal history disclosure form, including the Ohio supplement, exhibits, attachments, and updates.

(B) Notwithstanding any other law, upon written request from a person, the commission shall provide the following information to the person except as provided in this chapter:

(1) The information provided under this chapter concerning a licensee or an applicant;

(2) The amount of the wagering tax and admission tax paid daily to the state by a licensed applicant or an operating agent; and

(3) A copy of a letter providing the reasons for the denial of an applicant's license or an operating agent's contract and a copy of a letter providing the reasons for the commission's refusal to allow an applicant to withdraw the applicant's application, but with confidential information redacted if that information is the reason for the denial or refusal to withdraw.

(C) The individual's name, the individual's place of employment, the individual's job title, and the individual's gaming experience that is provided for an individual who holds, held, or has applied for a license under this chapter is not confidential. The reason for denial or revocation of a license or for disciplinary action against the individual and information submitted by the individual for a felony waiver request is not confidential. The cover sheet completed by an applicant for a key gaming employee license under section 3772.13 of the Revised Code is not confidential.

(D) An individual who holds, held, or has applied for a license under this chapter may waive the confidentiality requirements of division (A) of this section.

(E) Confidential information received by the commission from another jurisdiction relating to a person who holds, held, or has applied for a license under this chapter is confidential and not subject to disclosure as a public record under section 149.43 of the Revised Code. The commission may share the information referenced in this division with, or disclose the information to, the inspector general, any appropriate prosecuting authority, any law enforcement agency, or any other appropriate governmental or licensing agency, if the agency that receives the information complies with the same requirements regarding confidentiality as those with which the commission must comply.

Sec. 3772.17. (A) The upfront license fee to obtain a license as a casino operator shall be fifty million dollars per casino facility and shall be paid upon each casino operator's filing of its casino operator license application with the commission. The upfront license fee, once paid to the commission, shall be deposited into the economic development programs fund, which is created in the state treasury.

(B) New casino operator, management company, and holding company license and renewal license fees shall be set by rule. If an applicant for a license as a management company or holding company is related through a joint venture or controlled by or under common control with another applicant for a license as a casino operator, management company, or holding company for the same casino facility and the applicant for a license as a management company or holding company was reviewed for suitability as part of the investigation of the casino operator, only one license fee shall be assessed against both applicants for that casino facility.

(C) The fee to obtain an application for a casino operator, management company, or holding company license shall be one million five hundred thousand dollars per application. The application fee for a casino operator, management company, or holding company license may be increased to the extent that the actual review and investigation costs relating to an applicant exceed the application fee set forth in this division. If an applicant for a license as a management company or holding company is related through a joint venture or controlled by or under common control with another applicant for a license as a casino operator, management company, or holding company for the same casino facility, with the exception of actual costs of the review and investigation of the additional applicant, only one application fee shall be required of such applicants for that casino facility. The application fee shall be deposited into the casino control commission fund. The application fee is nonrefundable.

(D) The license fees for a gaming-related vendor shall be set by rule. Additionally, the commission may assess an applicant a reasonable fee in the amount necessary to process a gaming-related vendor license application.

(E) The license fees for a key employee shall be set by rule. Additionally, the commission may assess an applicant a reasonable fee in the amount necessary to process a key employee license application. If the license is being sought at the request of a casino operator, such fees shall be paid by the casino operator.

(F) The license fees for a casino gaming employee shall be set by rule. If the license is being sought at the request of a casino operator, the fee shall be paid by the casino operator.

Sec. 3772.18. (A) Each casino operator, management company, and holding company involved in the application and ownership or management of a casino facility shall provide to the commission as applicable:

(1) An annual balance sheet;

(2) An annual income statement;

(3) An annual audited financial statement;

(4) A list of the stockholders or other persons having at least a five per cent ownership interest in the casino operator, management company, or holding company and any other information the commission considers necessary for the effective administration of this chapter;

(5) Notification of any material changes to the applicant's or licensee's stockholders must be provided to the commission within sixty days of the change. Notification of any refinancing and debt issuance shall be in accordance with rules adopted by the commission under Chapter 119. of the Revised Code; and

(6) An applicant's compulsive and problem gambling plan. A casino operator shall submit an annual summary of its compulsive and problem gambling plan to the commission. The plan at a minimum shall contain the following elements:

(a) The goals of the plan and procedures and timetables to implement the plan;

(b) The identification of the individual who will be responsible for the implementation and maintenance of the plan;

(c) Policies and procedures including the following:

(i) The commitment of the casino operator to train appropriate employees;

(ii) The duties and responsibilities of the employees designated to implement or participate in the plan;

(iii) The responsibility of patrons with respect to responsible gambling;

(iv) Procedures for providing information to individuals regarding community, public and private treatment services, gamblers anonymous programs, and similar treatment or addiction therapy programs designed to prevent, treat, or monitor compulsive and problem gamblers and to counsel family members;

(v) The provision of printed material to educate patrons about compulsive and problem gambling and to inform them about treatment services available to compulsive and problem gamblers and their families;

(vi) The employee training program;

(vii) Procedures to prevent underage gambling;

(viii) Procedures to prevent intoxicated patrons from gambling;

(ix) The plan for posting signs within the casino facility containing gambling treatment information.

(B) Each casino operator shall submit quarterly updates and an annual report to the commission of its adherence to the plans and goals submitted under division (A) of this section.

(C) Preference shall be given to each of the following to train employees for casino-related employment opportunities:

(1) State institutions of higher education as defined in section 3345.011 of the Revised Code;

(2) Private career schools holding program authorizations issued by the state board of career colleges and schools under division (C) of section 3332.05 of the Revised Code;

(3) Private institutions exempt from regulation under Chapter 3332. of the Revised Code as prescribed in section 3333.046 of the Revised Code.

Sec. 3772.221. (A) Before a patron places a wager with any of the following persons over the internet, the person shall require the patron to establish a wagering account with the person and place all wagers with the person through that account:

(1) A permit holder;

(2)

An internet gambling operator;

(3) A sports gaming proprietor.

(B) A wagering account shall include all of the following information, which the person described in division (A) of this section shall update whenever the person becomes aware of a change:

(1) The patron's full legal name;

(2) The patron's date of birth;

(3) The patron's primary address;

(4) The patron's wagering account number or user name;

(5) If the person examined the patron's government-issued identification under division (C)(1) of this section, the type of identification examined, the identification number, and a digital copy of the identification;

(6) The method and any other information used to verify the patron's identity;

(7) The date of identity verification;

(8) A history of the patron's transactions with the person.

(C) Before creating a wagering account, a person described in division (A) of this section shall verify and document the patron's identity by doing one of the following:

(1) Examining the patron's government-issued identification, including the use of verification software designed to confirm the authenticity of the identification;

(2) Conducting multi-source authentication, which may include third party and governmental databases, as approved by the executive director.

(D) The person described in division (A) of this section shall require the patron, upon creating a wagering account, to do both of the following:

(1) Certify that the information provided to the person is accurate and that the patron is not excluded from or otherwise prohibited from wagering with the person. The person shall document the certification.

(2) Acknowledge the applicable minimum age to participate in wagering with the person and that the patron is prohibited from allowing any other person to access or use the patron's wagering account. The person shall document the acknowledgment.

(E) A person described in division (A) of this section shall implement procedures to ensure that wagering accounts comply with this section and with other applicable laws and rules, including ensuring, through commercially reasonable means, that all of the following are true:

(1) An individual who is prohibited from placing a wager with the person is not permitted to create a wagering account.

(2) An individual who becomes prohibited from placing a wager with the person is not permitted to continue to place wagers with the person while the individual is prohibited from doing so.

(3) A patron's identity is re-verified whenever reasonable suspicion exists that the patron's identification or wagering account has been compromised.

(4) The person complies with applicable state and federal requirements and industry standards for protecting the privacy and security of the person's patrons and their wagering accounts.

(F) A wagering account described in this section may allow the patron to place wagers with the person described in division (A) of this section by funding the wagering account through advance deposits. If the account does so, the person shall do all of the following:

(1) Allow the patron to fund the account only by the following means, in accordance with the person's house rules:

(a) Cash or vouchers deposited at an approved cashiering or kiosk location;

(b) Credit or debit card;

(c) Promotional credit;

(d) Winnings;

(e) Corrections made by the person with documented notification to the patron;

(f) Automated clearing house transfer;

(g) Wire transfer;

(h) Any other means approved by the executive director.

(2) Notify the patron of the establishment of a wagering account via electronic mail or regular mail;

(3) Apply a default deposit and wager limit on the account of five hundred dollars per week, subject to adjustment by the patron;

(4) Apply a default time limit of five hours per week spent using the person's web site or application through the account, subject to adjustment by the patron;

(5) Allow the patron, through an easy and obvious method, to impose additional limitations on the patron's wagering. The person shall comply with all of the following:

(a) Upon receiving any self-imposed limitation request, the person shall ensure that all specified limits are correctly implemented immediately or at the point in time that was clearly indicated by the patron.

(b) The patron's self-imposed limitations shall not override any more restrictive limitation imposed by the person.

(c) Once established by a patron and implemented by the person, it shall be possible to reduce the severity of a self-imposed limitation only upon the expiration of the self-imposed period.

(d) The person shall provide the patron with an option to set automatically renewing self-imposed limits.

(6) Require the patron to provide all of the following additional information, which the person shall update each time the person becomes aware of changes:

(a) The patron's telephone number;

(b) The patron's electronic mail address;

(c) The patron's social security number, the last four digits of the patron's social security number, or an equivalent identification number for a noncitizen patron, such as a passport or taxpayer identification number.

(7) Require the patron to protect access to the account using multi-factor authentication or a similar authentication method, as approved by the executive director;

(8) Prohibit a patron from transferring funds from one wagering account to another;

(9) Process a patron's request to withdraw the funds in the account within five business days, regardless of whether the account is open or closed, provided that if the person believes in good faith that the patron engaged in fraudulent conduct or other conduct that would cause the person to violate the law, the person may delay the withdrawal of funds to investigate or otherwise comply with the law. In such a case, the person shall provide notice to the patron of the general nature of the investigation of the account and conduct the investigation in a reasonable and expedient fashion, providing the patron with additional written notice of the status of the investigation at least every tenth business day starting from the day the original notice is provided to the patron.

(10) Refund any balance remaining in a wagering account closed by a patron in accordance with division (F)(9) of this section;

(11) Require that any manual addition or subtraction to or from the account of five hundred dollars or less be reviewed by supervisory personnel and that any other manual addition or subtraction to or from the account be authorized in advance by supervisory personnel.

(G) A person described in division (A) of this section shall provide each patron with all of the following:

(1) Notice of available responsible gaming resources;

(2) A readily accessible method for closing an account through the person's web site or application or upon contact with the person's customer service team;

(3) On-demand access to a summary statement of all of the patron's wagering activity with the person during the past year, accessible through the person's web site or application;

(4) Access, upon request, to a summary statement of all of the patron's wagering activity with the person during the past five years, accessible through the person's web site or application;

(5) A copy of the person's privacy policy governing the person's use and storage of patrons' confidential information.

Sec. 3772.23. (A) All tokens, chips, or electronic cards that are used to make wagers shall be purchased from the casino operator or management company while at a casino facility that has been approved by the commission. Chips, tokens, tickets, electronic cards, or similar objects may be used while at the casino facility only for the purpose of making wagers on casino games.

(B) Casino operators and management companies may provide promotional gaming credits or bonuses to their patrons in accordance with section 3775.231 of the Revised Code and the rules of the commission. Promotional gaming credits shall be subject to oversight by the commission.

(C) Casino operators and management companies shall not do any of the following:

(1) Obtain a license to operate a check-cashing business under sections 1315.01 to 1315.30 of the Revised Code;

(2) Obtain a license to provide loans under sections 1321.01 to 1321.19 of the Revised Code;

(3) Obtain a license to provide loans under sections 1321.35 to 1321.48 of the Revised Code.

Sec. 3772.231. (A) The requirements of this section apply to promotional gaming credits or bonuses issued by any of the following persons:

(1) A permit holder;

(2) A video lottery sales agent;

(3) An internet gambling operator;

(4) A casino operator or management company;

(5) A sports gaming proprietor.

(B) A person described in division (A) of this section may issue promotional gaming credits or bonuses to patrons only in accordance with this section and with the rules of the commission.

(C) A person described in division (A) of this section shall adopt procedures for issuing, accepting, and tracking all promotional gaming credits and bonuses and shall adopt rules governing them. The person shall make the rules available to its patrons and to the commission. The rules shall be clear and unambiguous and shall include all of the following:

(1) The date and time the promotion or bonus is active and expires;

(2) The rules of play;

(3) The nature and value of prizes or awards;

(4) All eligibility restrictions or limitations;

(5) All wagering and redemption requirements, including any limitations;

(6) All eligible events or wagers;

(7) All cancellation requirements;

(8) Terms and conditions that are full, accurate, concise, transparent, and do not contain misleading information.

(D)(1) Any promotional gaming credit or bonus that a person described in division (A) of this section describes as free or risk-free shall not require the patron to incur any loss or risk the patron's own money to use or withdraw winnings from the free wager.

(2) A promotional gaming credit or bonus may require the patron to play the credit or bonus through in order to withdraw it.

(3) A promotional gaming credit or bonus shall not restrict the patron from withdrawing the patron's own funds or withdrawing winnings from wagers placed using the patron's own funds.

(E)(1) A person described in division (A) of this section may offer a promotional gaming credit or bonus in connection with, or as a result of, a nongaming consumer transaction only if all of the following apply:

(a) The promotion or bonus does not target individuals under the minimum age to participate in the applicable form of gaming, other individuals who are ineligible to participate in that gaming, individuals with gambling problems, or other vulnerable individuals.

(b) The promotion or bonus is offered only to individuals who have been verified as having attained the minimum age to participate in the applicable form of gaming and as not being participants in the Ohio voluntary exclusion program.

(c) The promotion or bonus includes an opt out method that meets the requirements of division (D) of section 3772.41 of the Revised Code.

(2) Division (E)(1) of this section does not prohibit a person described in division (A) of this section from operating or participating in a consumer loyalty program approved by the executive director.

(F) Upon the occurrence of either of the following, a person described in division (A) of this section immediately shall stop offering a promotional gaming credit or bonus:

(1) The person becomes aware that the promotion or bonus is in violation of this section or of other applicable provisions of the Revised Code regarding gaming or the rules of the commission.

(2) The executive director of the commission orders the person to stop offering the promotion or bonus because it fails to comply with this section or with other applicable provisions of the Revised Code or the rules of the commission, or because it otherwise undermines the integrity of gaming.

Sec. 3772.28. (A) All of the following persons shall adopt and implement house rules in accordance with this section:

(1) A permit holder;

(2) A video lottery sales agent;

(3) An internet gambling operator;

(4) A casino operator or management company;

(5) A sports gaming proprietor.

(B) House rules adopted under division (A) of this section shall address all of the following:

(1) The types of wagers accepted;

(2) The method for calculating and paying winning wagers;

(3) The effect of scheduling changes or cancellations of horse races, sporting events, or other events, as applicable;

(4) The process for handling incorrectly posted horse racing or sporting events, odds, or results, if applicable;

(5) The method of notifying patrons of odds or proposition changes, if applicable;

(6) The methods by which a wager or wagering account may be funded;

(7) The methods for redeeming a winning wager;

(8) A policy regarding lost or damaged betting slips or tickets, if applicable;

(9) A requirement that any winning betting slip or ticket expire one year after the date the ticket outcome is determined, if applicable;

(10) Any process for accepting wagers at other than the posted terms;

(11) A process for canceling wagers for obvious errors, including notification of the patron;

(12) A process for patrons to contact the person to submit questions or complaints;

(13) An explanation of the process to resolve any dispute with a patron in accordance with division (E) of this section;

(14) A notification to patrons regarding the Ohio voluntary exclusion program;

(15) Any other matter required by the executive director of the commission.

(C)(1) A person described in division (A) of this section shall submit its house rules and any proposed change to its house rules to the commission in the manner and form required by the executive director for approval before implementing them.

(2) In the case of a proposed change, if the person does not receive a response from the executive director regarding the change within five days after submitting it, the change is considered to be approved.

(3) The executive director may, at any time, require a change to a person's house rules to ensure compliance with applicable laws or rules.

(D) A person described in division (A) of this section shall do all of the following:

(1) Make its current house rules available to the commission in a manner prescribed by the executive director;

(2) Display its current house rules, together with any other information the commission considers appropriate, conspicuously in each track, casino facility, or sports gaming facility and in any other place or manner prescribed by the commission;

(3) Make copies of its current house rules readily available to patrons.

(E)(1) Whenever a person described in division (A) of this section refuses payment of alleged winnings to a patron or there is otherwise a dispute with a patron regarding the patron's wagering account, wagers, wins, or losses, and the person and the patron are unable to resolve the dispute to the satisfaction of the patron, the person shall notify the patron of the patron's right to file a written complaint, the procedure for filing a written complaint, and the person's complaint resolution process. The notice requirement of this division is satisfied if the person directs the patron to information available on the person's web site or mobile application.

(2) Upon receiving a written complaint under division (E)(1) of this section, the person shall investigate and provide a written response to the patron within ten business days. If the person needs additional time to investigate or resolve the complaint beyond the ten business days, the person shall notify the patron of the need for additional time and give the patron an expected time frame in which the complaint may be resolved. The person's ultimate response may include a statement that if the dispute is not resolved to the satisfaction of the patron, the patron may submit the patron's complaint in writing to the commission.

Sec. 3772.281. (A) All of the following persons shall adopt procedures designed to protect the integrity of each form of gaming the person conducts, in accordance with this section:

(1) A permit holder;

(2) A video lottery sales agent;

(3) An internet gambling operator;

(4) A casino operator or management company;

(5) A sports gaming proprietor.

(B)(1) A person described in division (A) of this section shall submit its integrity procedures and any proposed change to its integrity procedures to the commission in the manner and form required by the executive director for approval before implementing them.

(2) In the case of a proposed change, if the person does not receive a response from the executive director regarding the change within five days after submitting it, the change is considered to be approved.

(3) The executive director may, at any time, require a change to a person's integrity procedures to ensure compliance with applicable laws or rules.

(C) A person described in division (A) of this section shall make its current integrity procedures available to the commission at all times.

Sec. 3772.31. (A) The commission, by and through the executive director of the commission and as required under section 125.05 of the Revised Code, may enter into contracts necessary to ensure the proper operation and reporting of all casino gaming authorized under this chapter. The commission shall not require use of a central system by a casino operator if the casino operator is in compliance with this chapter. If the commission determines, after written notice to the casino operator and a hearing under section 3772.04 of the Revised Code, that a casino operator is not in compliance with this chapter, the commission may determine it is necessary to require the casino operator to install and implement a central system under such conditions as the commission may require. Before any such hearing, the commission shall provide the casino operator with written notice that the casino operator is not in compliance with a specific requirement of this chapter, describe the requirement, and provide the casino operator at least thirty days to cure the noncompliance or, if the cure cannot be reasonably rectified within thirty days, require the casino operator to demonstrate to the commission's satisfaction that the casino operator is diligently pursuing the required cure. The system shall be operated by or under the commission's control. If the commission determines that a central system is necessary and adopts rules authorizing a central system, casino operators shall be responsible for the costs of the central system as it relates to casino facilities.

(B) The commission shall certify independent testing laboratories to scientifically test and technically evaluate all slot machines, mechanical, electromechanical, or electronic table games, slot accounting systems, and other electronic gaming equipment for compliance with this chapter. The certified independent testing laboratories shall be accredited by a national accreditation body. The commission shall certify an independent testing laboratory if it is competent and qualified to scientifically test and evaluate electronic gaming equipment for compliance with this chapter and to otherwise perform the functions assigned to an independent testing laboratory under this chapter. An independent testing laboratory shall not be owned or controlled by, or have any interest in, a gaming-related vendor of electronic gaming equipment. The commission shall prepare a list of certified independent testing laboratories from which independent testing laboratories shall be chosen for all purposes under this chapter.

Sec. 3772.311. (A) The commission shall certify independent testing laboratories to scientifically test and technically evaluate all of the following for compliance with applicable laws and the rules of the commission:

(1) All electronic instant bingo systems, pursuant to section 3768.12 of the Revised Code;

(2) All totalizator equipment and other equipment used in the operation of pari-mutuel wagering on horse racing under Chapter 3769. of the Revised Code;

(3) All video lottery terminals;

(4) All internet gambling equipment and all forms, variations, or composites of internet gambling;

(5) All slot machines, mechanical, electromechanical, or electronic table games, slot accounting systems, and other electronic gaming equipment;

(6) All sports gaming equipment and all forms, variations, or composites of sports gaming.

(B)(1) An independent testing laboratory shall apply to the commission, on a form prescribed by the commission, for certification with respect to the testing and evaluation of each type of equipment described in division (A) of this section that the laboratory wishes to be certified to test. The laboratory shall pay the commission a nonrefundable certification fee in an amount determined by the commission by rule, sufficient to pay the cost of processing the application.

(2) A certification issued under this section is valid for a term of five years. The laboratory may apply to renew its certification in the same manner as for initial certification not later than ninety days before the certification expires. If the commission has investigated the laboratory for compliance under this section within the past year, the executive director of the commission may apply the results of the previous compliance investigation to the renewal application.

(3) The laboratory shall pay all costs of its compliance investigation for purposes of an initial or renewed certification under this section. The investigation may include a review of all of the following:

(a) The laboratory's business structure, including its ownership and controlling interests;

(b) The laboratory's policies and procedures, to determine whether they prevent conflicts of interest, provide for segregation of duties, detect and prevent fraud, and ensure impartiality;

(c) The laboratory's staff's experience and expertise;

(d) The sufficiency of the laboratory's staff, equipment, and test scripts;

(e) The laboratory's financial viability;

(f) The laboratory's compliance with division (C) of this section and the rules of the commission.

(C) In order to be certified, an independent testing laboratory shall meet all of the following requirements:

(1) It shall be competent and qualified to scientifically test and evaluate the applicable equipment for compliance with applicable laws and rules and to otherwise perform the functions assigned to an independent testing laboratory under this chapter.

(2) It shall be accredited by a national accreditation body to international organization for standardization (ISO) standards 17020 and 17025 and any other standards as required by the executive director.

(3) It shall be independent of any entity or product for which it provides testing services.

(4) It shall maintain professional liability (errors and omissions) insurance of not less than one million dollars.

(5) It shall employ a full-time quality manager who is responsible for ensuring compliance with national accreditation standards and applicable laws and rules.

(6) It shall maintain physical security at each laboratory facility, including surveillance systems and alarms, to minimize the risk that confidential information is misappropriated.

(7) It shall maintain the information technology security necessary to minimize the risk that confidential information is misappropriated.

(8) It shall utilize the verification methodologies required by the executive director. The laboratory shall request approval from the executive director to use alternative verification methodologies. If approved, the laboratory shall provide the verification tool utilizing the alternative verification methodologies to the commission.

(9) It shall ensure that testing and support procedures are performed consistently at all laboratory locations.

(10) It shall make available to the commission, upon request, all its policies, procedures, and records.

(11) It shall make available to the commission, upon request, a master list of all test methods, standards, forms, and other relevant documents it uses.

(12) It shall not subcontract any testing without the prior written approval of the executive director.

(13) It shall maintain all records for a minimum of five years unless otherwise specified, as follows:

(a) It shall maintain, for a minimum of five years after an associated commission approval is no longer in effect, an electronic database containing testing data, reports, and related materials and provide access to the commission. Data, reports, and related materials shall be provided in a format and through a mechanism required by the commission.

(b) If not available from the manufacturer, it shall maintain an inventory of gaming equipment and versions of software tested by the laboratory and approved by the commission for a minimum of five years after an associated commission approval is no longer in effect.

(c) It shall maintain, for a minimum of five years after the end of the laboratory's certification period in which the submission occurred, all software and associated documentation and source code submitted for testing but not subsequently approved by the commission. The information shall be stored in a manner that is secure and readily accessible to appropriately approved laboratory personnel.

(14) It shall assist the commission in investigations when requested, at the expense of the laboratory. The laboratory may seek reimbursement from the owner of the devices subject to investigation.

(15) When requested, it shall assist or train commission agents in accounting, auditing, compliance, security, technology, and verification practices for any submission to that laboratory seeking approval from the commission.

(16) It shall meet any additional condition imposed by the commission at a meeting held under section 3772.02 of the Revised Code.

(D) A certified independent testing laboratory shall do all of the following:

(1) Provide a recommendation as to whether any equipment submitted to it for testing complies with the applicable laws and rules;

(2) Provide a report to the commission, in a format approved by the commission, of its test results for each submission to be considered for approval by the commission;

(3) Testify at any administrative hearing or court proceeding as requested by the commission;

(4) Annually review each of its employees, including conducting a criminal background check and an assessment of the employee's training, experience, performance, and competence;

(5) Take corrective action whenever any nonconforming work is discovered, laboratory procedures are not followed, procedures are required to be changed, or other unsatisfactory conditions exist. The quality manager shall file an internal report with the laboratory, describing why the problem occurred, what was done to resolve the problem, and how the problem will be prevented in the future. The laboratory shall make the report available to the commission upon request.

(6) Establish and maintain a training program for its employees to ensure the employees maintain the experience and expertise to conduct all testing required by the commission. The laboratory shall maintain training records for all employees and make them available to the commission upon request.

(7) Test and verify that all testing equipment and software used internally is suitable for use. The laboratory shall have internal policies to verify that the equipment and software will perform as needed. The laboratory shall maintain records of test cases involving the validation of equipment, software, and methods. All testing performed by the laboratory shall be traceable to a test method that has been suitably validated.

(8) Notify the executive director immediately if it fails to maintain compliance with any requirement of division (C) of this section.

(E) The commission shall maintain a list of certified independent testing laboratories from which independent testing laboratories shall be chosen for all purposes under this chapter.

Sec. 3772.312. (A)(1) A licensee that seeks the approval of a piece of equipment or a game described in division (A) of section 3772.311 of the Revised Code shall do all of the following:

(a) Submit a written request to a certified independent testing laboratory that identifies the equipment or game and, at a minimum, specifically references the scientific testing and technical evaluation necessary to determine compliance with the applicable laws and rules;

(b) Submit all necessary items and information to the laboratory and, if requested by the commission, to the commission;

(c) Pay all costs associated with the testing and evaluation.

(2) A licensee shall engage not more than one certified independent testing laboratory to test and evaluate any particular version of a piece of equipment or any particular version of a game, unless the executive director of the commission gives prior written authorization for testing and evaluation by an additional laboratory.

(B) Except as otherwise approved by the executive director of the commission, no equipment or game described in division (A) of section 3772.311 of the Revised Code shall be used in this state unless it has been tested by a certified independent testing laboratory and the executive director has certified the test results. The commission shall publish a catalog of all approved equipment and games.

(C) Any equipment or game described in division (A) of section 3772.311 of the Revised Code, or any component of any such equipment or game, that the commission deems to be critical shall be capable of verification using a verification method approved by the executive director.

(D) The executive director may require that a previously approved piece of equipment or game be retested under this section if the executive director determines that the equipment or game fails to meet any applicable requirement of law or rule or jeopardizes the integrity of gaming in any way.

Sec. 3772.313. (A) Both of the following systems shall meet the requirements of this section:

(1)

An internet gambling system;

(2) Sports gaming equipment used to accept wagers on sporting events.

(B) A system described in division (A) of this section shall meet all of the following requirements:

(1) It shall monitor and record all gaming-related data in real time, including any changes made to the data or system.

(2) It shall provide accurate reporting using a method and format approved by the executive director.

(3) It shall be capable of automatically preparing a report summarizing the results of all wagering transactions conducted in this state by the permit holder, internet gambling operator, or sports gaming proprietor. The reporting function shall be tested by a certified independent testing laboratory under section 3772.312 of the Revised Code to confirm that it accurately calculates and displays the results of wagering.

(4) The executive director of the commission shall determine the format and required periods of the report.

(5) The servers or other equipment responsible for accepting wagers shall meet all of the following requirements:

(a) They shall be located in this state, provided that data may be stored or accessed elsewhere, including in a cloud based environment.

(b) They shall be managed by a person that holds an appropriate license issued by the commission for that purpose.

(c) The data center where they are housed shall be secure and shall have controls in place to prevent unauthorized access.

(6) The system shall utilize disk redundancy, and the data shall be backed up to prevent the loss of data and to minimize down time.

(7) All communication with the system shall be secured utilizing an encryption methodology that ensures data integrity and prevents data theft.

(8) The system shall perform an authentication check on any gaming equipment that connects to it and shall not accept any wagers or player account requests from gaming equipment that fails the authentication check. The authentication check shall meet all of the following requirements:

(a) It shall occur at least once every twenty-four hours.

(b) It shall determine, with a high degree of accuracy, whether the gaming equipment has been altered in a way that may threaten the integrity of the system or its data.

(c) It shall log all of the following information:

(i) The date and time;

(ii) A device identifier;

(iii) The device type;

(iv) The device's location;

(v) The disposition of the authentication check.

(9) It shall be designed to prevent and detect all of the following:

(a) Unauthorized use of an account by someone other than the account holder;

(b) Unauthorized withdrawals from patron accounts;

(c) Wagering by any person who is prohibited from making a wager, including any person who is underage, a participant in the Ohio voluntary exclusion program, or a person who is on an involuntary exclusion list.

(C) A permit holder, internet gambling operator, or sports gaming proprietor shall do all of the following:

(1) Provide read-only remote access to its systems, and any hardware or software required to facilitate that access, to the commission in a manner prescribed by the executive director;

(2) Pay all costs associated with providing access to the commission under division (C)(1) of this section;

(3) Test the system's recovery procedures on a sample basis at least annually, document the results, and make them available to the commission upon request;

(4) Implement change management policies and procedures for tracking and controlling changes made to the system, including all of the following:

(a) Procedures for testing proposed changes in a non-production environment. The non-production environment shall be segregated from the production environment and shall not have the capability to alter data in the production environment.

(b) Procedures for classifying changes as follows:

(i) High impact, meaning any change to, or addition of, components that impact the operational integrity of the system. As used in this division, "components" includes any component deemed to be critical by the certified independent testing laboratory.

(ii) Low impact, meaning any other change to the system.

(c) Procedures for the installation of changes into the production environment;

(d) Procedures for the rollback of changes;

(e) Procedures for documenting each phase of the change management process;

(f) Procedures for logging all changes to the system;
